segments.s110-g105: زمانه (subject) 'time, the age' comes first, followed by the adverbial phrase بدین سان (a fixed lit. compound meaning 'in this way/thus'). همی is the archaic literary particle marking a continuing or habitual present; together همی بگذرد is a present/habitual verbal phrase 'keeps passing' or 'continues to pass.' Word order is Subject — adverbial — verbal phrase, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s110-g106: دمش is the object with the enclitic -ش ('its'), here meaning 'its moments' or 'its breaths' referring back to زمانه. مرد دانا is the noun plus post‑nominal adjective 'a wise man' (Persian adjectives normally follow the noun). همی before the verb again marks habitual/continuing action; بشمرد (ب + شمرد) is the present/habitual form 'counts/reckons.' The phrase literally orders object — subject — verb, a permissible poetic arrangement that emphasizes 'its moments.'
segments.s110-g107: هران روز بد is a noun phrase 'each/that bad day' (هرآن = 'each/that'), followed by a relative clause introduced by کز 'which/that' + the pronoun تو 'you'. اندر گذشت is a literary past construction meaning 'passed (away)'; the relative clause therefore reads 'that passed because of/with you.' The relative clause follows the head noun, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s110-g108: بر آنی is a prepositional phrase 'upon/at that instant' (بر = 'upon/on', آنی = 'instant/moment' used nominally). کزو is the relative 'from which' linking to the following clause; گیتی آباد گشت is a past‑tense result clause 'the world became prosperous' (گشت = 'became'). Together the segment means 'at that instant from which the world became prosperous.'
segments.s110-g33: همانا is an emphatic, archaic particle meaning 'verily' or 'truly'. که here introduces the content clause that follows ('that/for which'); از بهر is a two-word prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' (بهر = 'for'), and این روزگار = 'this time/these times' (an adverbial phrase giving purpose or occasion). Word order is poetic: the explanatory phrase 'همانا که از بهر این روزگار' sets up the cause/purpose before the main clause.
segments.s110-g34: This is a VSO/OV-inverted poetic line: پرورانید is an archaic past form 'nurtured/raised' (the verb), ترا = ت + را is the clitic object 'you' with the object marker, and پروردگار is the agent 'the Lord' placed after the verb for emphasis. In modern Persian order it would be 'پروردگار ترا پرورانید' → 'the Lord reared you.'
segments.s110-g35: نشاید is an impersonal archaic negative modal meaning 'it is not fitting/it ought not to be'. بدین is split in the orthography here (ب + دین) but functions as 'in/with this' (بدین = 'in this/with this'), and کار آهرمنی = 'Ahrimanic work/deed' (آهرمنی = demonic, adjectival). Together the phrase says this action is unbecoming/unsuitable for such demonic conduct.
segments.s110-g36: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the previous judgment ('that ...'). آسایش آری is a poetic clause meaning 'that you obtain/receive rest/peace' (آسایش = 'peace/comfort'); آری here is an older/formal verbal/formulaic use (not the simple 'yes') and functions to indicate obtaining/coming (poetic ambiguity). و‌گر = 'and if / but if', دم = 'breath' (i.e. to be alive/inhale) and زنی here is the second-person verb form 'you draw/you breathe' in older diction (not the noun 'woman'); the clause sets up a hypothetical 'even if you breathe' contrasting with 'you shall not have rest.'
segments.s110-g37: برت را = 'your bearing/reputation' or literally 'upon you' with را marking the direct object (برت is poetic/archaic for one's mien/reputation here). به ببر = 'to the tiger' (به = 'to/toward'), بیان = 'expression/statement', سخت = 'harsh/strong', and کن is the imperative 'do (make)'. The clause is an imperative sequence telling the addressee to present themselves strongly to the tiger (figuratively: to a fierce foe).
segments.s110-g38: This is a second imperative clause joined to the previous: سر از خواب = 'lift (your) head from sleep' or 'wake up', و اندیشه = 'and thought/consideration', پردخت (classical spelling of پرداخت) here means 'set about/attend to / apply' (i.e. give attention or gird oneself), and کن is again the imperative 'do'. Together: 'Awaken from sleep and apply thought/resolve.'
segments.s110-g39: هران تن is an archaic form of هر آن تن = 'every person/each man'. که introduces the relative clause 'who/that', چشمش = 'his eye' (possessive clitic), سنان = 'spear tip/point', تو = 'your' (address), and دید = past 'saw/beheld'. Word order is relative: 'every man who his eye saw (the) point of you' → 'everyone who saw the tip of your spear.'
segments.s110-g40: که introduces reported speech/prediction: گوید here is a poetic/future-oriented or gnomic use meaning 'will say' or 'one will say' (the provided dictionary senses list is for 'conversation' rather than the verbal use, so treat it as the verb 'to say' in context). The second که is the 'that' of reported speech; او را روان آرمید = 'his soul/spirit (روان) is at rest/has departed' (آرمید is perfect/past 'rested' used here to mean 'his spirit has left him'). The whole clause reports what such a man will declare after seeing the spear.
segments.s110-g17: هم ... هم is the correlative 'both ... and ...' linking two coordinated nouns; the first هم introduces the first member ('that treasure') and the second هم marks the second member ('that famed army'). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', and the adjective نامدار follows the noun لشکر in postpositive position as in Persian poetry.
segments.s110-g18: بیاراسته here functions as a predicate/adjective describing the two previous items; چون + simile + اندر بهار gives the comparison 'like a flower in spring.' Note the poetic order: the simile phrase follows the thing described. (If read as آراسته/‘adorned’ this is a positive simile; the supplied lemma senses did not include that reading.)
segments.s110-g19: همه 'all' is the subject/quantifier; چرخ گردان is a poetic compound 'the turning wheel/heavens' (i.e. the revolving world or fate), where گردان is an adjective 'turning/revolving.' به + دیوان marks the indirect object 'to the diwan/office,' and سپرد is the simple past 'he entrusted/handed over.'
segments.s110-g20: تو گویی is an impersonal or idiomatic expression 'one would say' / 'it seems as if' (here introducing a comparison or imagined remark); که introduces the subordinate clause; باد اندر آمد ببرد is a consecutive clause 'the wind came in to take (it) away' with آمد (past) + ببرد (subjunctive/optative sense of برد used to express what 'would' happen).
segments.s110-g21: چو + past verb (بشنید) = 'when he heard.' The following prepositional phrase بر تن ('on/over the body') precedes the verb بدرید پوست in poetic word order: object/phrase + verb + direct object (پوست = 'skin'). بدرید is past tense 'tore.'
segments.s110-g22: ز دشمن نهان داشت is a reversed word order for emphasis: 'he kept (it) hidden from the enemy' (ز = 'from,' نهان = 'hidden/secretly,' داشت = past 'kept/held'). این هم ز دوست appends 'this also from a friend,' where هم = 'also' and z دوست = 'from (his) friend,' i.e. 'he hid it both from the enemy and from his friend.'
segments.s110-g23: به روشن‌دل is a dative/benefactive phrase used poetically for the person 'the clear‑hearted one' (subject); از دور 'from afar' gives the vantage, and بدها (plural) + بدید is 'saw bad things/ill omens.' Word order places the dative/recipient-like phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s110-g24: که introduces an indirect question/clause: 'what will reach/come to the time (age) from now on.' زین بر here corresponds to archaic/poetic 'from this point on' (zīn/زین as a frozen phrase), بر زمانه = 'upon the age/world,' چه خواهد رسید = 'what will (خواهـد = future auxiliary) reach/happen.' Note that the supplied lemma for زین did not match this usage, so it is treated as the idiomatic 'from now/on.'
segments.s110-g73: ولیکن is a coordinating adversative 'but'. بدوزخ is a prefixed locution ('be-' + 'dozakh') meaning 'into hell' (literally 'to-hell'). چمیدن here is an infinitive/Verbal noun meaning 'to throw oneself/descend' (poetic usage); به + پای marks the directional/goal phrase 'toward the feet of...'. Word order is literary: adversative + directional infinitive phrase.
segments.s110-g74: This noun phrase and verb finish the clause. بزرگان پیشین is a genitival/attributive phrase 'the earlier/ancient great ones' (بزرگان = great ones; پیشین = earlier/ancient). ندیدند is the past-tense, 3rd-plural verb 'they did not see' (negated by the preceding context); رای is the object meaning 'counsel/plan/opinion' and follows the verb in this poetic SOV-flexible order.
segments.s110-g75: همان functions as 'such'/'that one'. از تن خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from his own body and temperament' (خویش = his disposition; ش is the pronominal clitic 'his'). نابوده is a predicative or adjectival past participle 'ruined/destroyed'. سیر here is an adjective/noun 'sated' (state of satiety) modifying the implied person; the whole phrase describes state/condition.
segments.s110-g76: نیاید is a negative-present/optative form 'does not come' or 'will not come' in literary register; کسی is the indefinite 'anyone' (the negative context makes it effectively 'no one'). پیش = 'before/in the presence of'. درنده شیر is a nominal compound 'the ravening/ferocious lion' (درنده adjectival; شیر = lion). The clause states that no such person approaches the lion.
segments.s110-g77: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. من is the emphatic subject 'I'. کمربسته is a compound adjective/past participle 'girded' (literally 'belt-fastened', i.e., ready). رفته‌گیر is a poetic adjectival compound meaning 'firmly resolved/tenacious' (an idiomatic quality). The two adjectives jointly describe the speaker's present state.
segments.s110-g78: نخواهم is the first-person negative of خواستن 'I will not want / I do not wish' (literary present/future sense). جز = 'except'. از introduces the source/agent ('from'). دادگر is an adjective/noun 'just, righteous' (here describing the helper). دستگیر is used as 'helper/succorer' (literally 'hand-taker' but functionally 'one who gives help'). Together the clause means 'I will accept help from no one except a just helper.'
segments.s110-g79: تن و جان is a fixed pairing 'body and life/soul'. فدای + (object) + کردن is a verbal compound meaning 'to sacrifice for / to give up in devotion to' (فدای سپهبد کردن = 'to sacrifice for the spahbed'). کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of کردن 'I will do / I will make', here rendering a vow or determined intention 'I will sacrifice.'
segments.s110-g80: طلسم دل جادوان is a possessive/attributive structure 'the spell (طلسم) on the heart (دل) of the enchanters (جادوان)' — jادوان here is a plural/collective 'magicians/enchanters'. بشکنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of 'to break' (شکستن) and expresses resolve 'I will break'. Grammatically the verb follows the object phrase, typical of Persian verbal construction.
segments.s110-g65: تواند is the 3rd-person singular present (modal) of توانستن, meaning 'can' or 'is able to'. The following word کسی is the delayed subject ('anyone'), and این سخن is the definite object 'this speech/utterance'; بازداشت is a verbal noun/past-stem used as the action 'to hold back/ restrain'. The clause therefore literally orders: 'Can anyone this speech hold back?' — a poetic word order (verb before subject and object).
segments.s110-g66: چنان is an adverb 'in such a way/so'. کاو is the archaic relative-like element (from که او) meaning 'he who/one who', introducing the conditional idea (here treated as 'someone who...'); since that sense wasn't in the provided list it is marked -1. گذارد is the simple past/Perfect-stem 3rd-sg 'placed/put' (here 'sets/places'); بباید is the impersonal/modal 'one must/it ought', and گذاشت is the infinitive/past-stem 'to put/ to leave' used as the complement of بباید. Together the clause reads roughly: 'If one places it thus, it must be placed.'
segments.s110-g67: نخواهد here functions as the future/modal auxiliary 'will not' (ن‑ prefix + خواهد), with همی before the verb giving continuous or emphatic aspect ('still/continually'); ماند is 'remain' and ایدر 'here'. کسی is the delayed subject 'anyone' following the verb. The order (verb + subject + adverbial) is typical of older/poetic Persian: 'It/itself will not remain here, anyone.'
segments.s110-g68: بخوانند is 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form of خواندن ('they read/recite' or generically 'people will read/recite'). اگرچه is the concessive conjunction 'although/even if'. بماند is a present/subjunctive of ماندن 'it may remain' (used concessively here), and بسی is an adverb 'much/a great deal'. The clause says: 'they will read it, even if it remains a long time.'
segments.s110-g69: کسی here is 'someone/no one' depending on context ('no one who...'); کاو again functions as the archaic relative 'that/he who' (not listed among the provided senses, so marked -1). جهان را uses the object marker را after 'world' ('the world' as object), and بنام بلند is a compound: بنام 'by name/as named' (here 'with the name') + بلند 'lofty/high' qualifying that name. The whole phrase reads: 'someone who has made the world by a lofty name' (i.e. 'who made the world renowned by a lofty name').
segments.s110-g70: گذارد is the perfect/past-stem 3rd-sing 'placed/made' (here 'has made/has placed'); به is the preposition 'in/at/on/to' introducing the circumstance 'to/at departing', and رفتن in such contexts often means 'to die/ depart (this life)' (this sense is selected). نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن ('is not/there is not') used impersonally here — the precise negative sense ('is not') is not among the provided senses so it is given -1; نژند is the adjective 'base/without honour'. Together: 'he who made the world famed by a lofty name — in departing (dying) there will be no baseness (i.e. he will not be dishonoured in death).'
segments.s110-g71: چنین is the adverb 'thus/so' introducing reported speech. گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (the lemma senses given were nouns; the verbal use is the intended meaning and not among the provided senses, so it is marked -1). رستم is the speaker's name, and به فرخ پدر means 'to Farrokh, (my) father' — the dative preposition به introduces the addressee; پدر is 'father'. Word order is straightforward: 'Thus said Rostam to Farrokh, (his) father.'
segments.s110-g72: که introduces reported content 'that'. من is the first-person pronoun 'I'. بسته is an adjective/past participle 'bound/tied' (here 'girded' or 'fastened'); دارم is the present 'I have / I hold' (auxiliary sense). به + فرمان + کمر is a prepositional phrase 'by/at the command/order of the girdle/waist' — فرمان used as 'order/command' and کمر as 'girdle/belt'. The literal sense: 'that I have bound (myself), at the command of the belt.'
segments.s110-g41: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). جنگ دریا is a noun+modifier phrase meaning 'war of the sea' (noun before its modifier is common in poetry). کنی is the 2nd‑person singular subjunctive/conditional form of کردن used in the protasis ('if you do/make'), and خون شود is the apodosis: خون (object/phenomenon) + شود (3rd‑person subjunctive/optative of شدن) gives the result 'blood will be / blood would be shed.'
segments.s110-g42: از آوای تو is a causal/instrumental prepositional phrase ('from / because of your voice'); آوای is the genitive form (آوا + ـی) with تو. The clause core is کوه هامون شود where کوه is the thing affected and هامون (a plain) is the result‑state, with شود (become) as the 3rd‑person verb: 'the mountain will become a plain.' The phrase therefore states a caused transformation ('by your voice the mountain becomes a plain').
segments.s110-g43: نباید که is an impersonal modal negative meaning 'it should not be that / must not'; که introduces the subordinate clause. The following phrase ارژنگ و دیو سپید is the compound subject ('Arzhang and the white demon'), with the adjective سپید postposed to دیو in classical/poetic word order.
segments.s110-g44: به جان از تو دارند is the verbal clause: به جان ('with respect to the life' / 'concerning the life') + از تو ('of you') form the object of دارند ('they have'); دارند is present 3rd‑person plural 'they have / possess'. هرگز (never) negates امید (hope), which is the noun object: 'they never have hope regarding your life.' The word order (object phrase before دارند, adverb after the verb) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s110-g45: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed first for immediacy. گردن شاه مازندران is a genitive chain 'the neck of the king of Mazandaran' (گردن = neck; شاه مازندران = the king of Mazandaran); this noun phrase supplies the direct target of the imperative that follows.
segments.s110-g46: همه خرد بشکن is an imperative: همه خرد ('all wisdom' / 'all his sense') is the object and بشکن is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'break / shatter.' بگرز گران is an instrumental/adjunct phrase meaning 'with the heavy mace' where the initial b‑ is an archaic/poetic prefix (parallel to با) attached to گرز ('mace') and گران 'heavy' modifies it; the phrase indicates the instrument of the action. (Note: the listed lemma senses for بگرز do not match the weapon sense, so that item is treated as an archaic instrumental form here.)
segments.s110-g47: چنین پاسخش داد رستم is a compact clause 'Thus (چنین) he gave his reply, Rostam' — پاسخش is 'his reply' (پاسخ + enclitic ـش 'his/its'), داد is the past tense 'gave', and رستم is the subject (classical word order often places the subject after verb). که introduces the reported content ('that...').
segments.s110-g48: درازست is a predicate 'is long' describing راه ('the road'). و coordinates the next clause. من چون شوم uses چون in the temporal/conditional sense ('when/if I become') and شوم is the 1st‑person subjunctive/potential of شدن ('I become / I should become'). کینه خواه is a predicative nominal: کینه 'hatred, grudge' + خواه functioning as 'seeking / desirous' (here adjectival/nominal, 'vengeful' or 'one who seeks vengeance'); together the clause expresses 'if I become vengeful / if I am moved to hatred...' and implies the ensuing action of vengeance.
segments.s110-g25: به رستم = a prepositional phrase 'to Rostam' (به + proper name). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb گفت. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' coming before the subject in this poetic word order; دستان سام is a nominative phrase 'the mighty‑handed Sam' (دستان here used as an epithet 'of hands/strong‑handed' modifying the name سام). Together they form 'Sam (strong‑handed) said thus to Rostam.'
segments.s110-g26: که introduces a reported subordinate clause ('that...'). شمشیر کوته شد اندر نیام is a verbless/result clause rendered with شد ('became') — 'the sword became short' i.e. 'the sword has been shortened' — and اندر نیام 'in the sheath' (اندر = in/within, نیام = scabbard). The overall sense: 'that the sword has become shortened in its scabbard.'
segments.s110-g27: نشاید is an impersonal modal verb 'it is not fitting/should not.' کزین پس is a temporal phrase 'from this onward / henceforth' (کزین = from this, پس = after). چمیم و چریم are two short, archaic/poetic verbs describing idle/passive activity (here contrasted with taking action); they are used as activities one would do 'henceforth' and are intentionally colloquial/poetic rather than a literal modern verb form.
segments.s110-g28: وگر is the conditional 'or if / but if.' تخت را is the definite direct object 'the throne' (را marks definiteness). خویشتن is an emphatic reflexive 'ourselves'/'our own.' پروریم is a 1st‑person (plural) present/subjunctive or optative form of پروردن 'to rear/nurture' — here 'we should raise/nurture.' The clause means 'or if we were to rear the throne ourselves.'
segments.s110-g29: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/since/that.' شاه جهان is a title 'the king of the world' (word order: noun + epithet). در دم is an idiomatic locution 'in the breath/moment' meaning 'in imminent danger' (دم = breath/moment), and اژدهاست is a predicate 'is in the dragon' (poetically: 'is in the dragon's jaws/breath'), giving the image of extreme peril.
segments.s110-g30: به ایرانیان is a dative phrase 'for/to the Iranians.' بر here is the preposition 'upon/on' and introduces the predicate 'what ... is upon them.' چه مایه is an idiomatic interrogative 'what sort/what degree (of)...' مایه functions as 'cause/source' and بلاست is the predicate 'is calamity/affliction' (archaic/poetic). The line asks rhetorically what calamity has befallen the Iranians.
segments.s110-g31: کنون 'now' opens the sentence. کرد باید is a classical periphrastic expression meaning 'it must be done / you must' (کرد used as a light verb + باید 'must'). ت + را together mark the 2nd‑person object 'ترا' ('you'), split in the segment list; رخش زین places the horse name رخش and زین 'saddle' together meaning 'saddle Rakhsh' — object order reflects Persian noun phrase 'Rakhsh (the) saddle' i.e. 'saddle your Rakhsh.' The phrase expresses necessity/command to act now.
segments.s110-g32: بخواهی here is a subjunctive/optative/ hortative form meaning roughly 'take up / seize' (classical usage where خواهی can indicate the desired/necessary action). به + تیغ marks the instrument 'with the sword,' جهان‑بخش is a compound adjectival epithet 'world‑bestowing' modifying تیغ, and کین is a noun 'vengeance' linked to تیغ (a sword of vengeance). The clause instructs to take up that particular sword.
segments.s110-g49: ازین پادشاهی literally 'az-in pādshāhi' is an archaic folded prepositional phrase: ازین = از + این ('of/from this', here 'concerning this'), پادشاهی = 'kingship'. بد�ان گفت زال is an inverted poetic order: بدان ('to that/thereupon' or 'thus/thereof') + گفت (said) + زال (subject after the verb). So the clause means 'Zal said this concerning the kingship.' Note the poetic contraction ازین (از این) and the verb preceding the subject, both common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s110-g50: دو راهست is the compressed phrase دو راه است ('there are two ways'); راه (way) + است (copula) is condensed into راهست. هر دو = 'both (of them)'. به here marks accompaniment/qualification ('with/regarding'), so هر دو به رنج و وبال means 'both of them are accompanied by suffering and burden.' The nouns رنج و وبال form a coordinate object of the prepositional phrase.
segments.s110-g51: یکی از دو راه = 'one of the two roads/ways' (numeric + iz-phrase). آنک is an archaic relative particle meaning 'that/which', introducing the relative clause آنک کاووس رفت = 'which Kavus went (took)'. Note the verb رفت (went) appears after the subject کاووس, typical Persian word order but with the relative particle up front.
segments.s110-g52: دگر = 'the other/another' and it governs a list of places: کوه و بالا و منزل دو هفت = 'mountain(s) and heights and the station(s) of two sevens.' The coordination with و links the nouns; منزل here is best read as 'stage/station (of a journey)'. دو هفت is a compound numeral phrase (two × seven) producing 'fourteen' in classical usage.
segments.s110-g53: پر از ... means 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)'). The phrase uses repeated پر از to emphasize contents: 'full of demons and (of) lion-like creatures, and full of darkness.' شیرست is an adjective 'lion-like/brave' modifying implied beasts, and تیرگی is 'darkness/gloom'. The copula 'است' is omitted here (poetic ellipsis), so these noun phrases describe the other road.
segments.s110-g54: بماند (remains/is left) is a verb meaning 'will remain' or 'is left,' placed before its arguments in poetic word order. بدو = 'toward it/to him' (directional), چشمت = 'your eye' (possessive), and از introduces cause: از خیرگی = 'from/with astonishment' or 'in a fixed stare.' The clause thus means 'your eye will remain fixed at it in astonishment.'
segments.s110-g55: تو is the emphatic 'you.' کوتاه بگزین is an imperative phrase 'choose briefly' (کوتاه as adverb/adjective before the verb بگزین, imperative of گزیدن). شگفتی ببین is a second imperative 'see/behold the marvel.' Two imperatives are strung together to give advice/command: 'You, choose briefly and behold the wonder.'
segments.s110-g56: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that.' یار تو باشد جهان‌آفرین is inverted but means 'that the world's Creator will be your companion/ally.' باشد is the subjunctive/present form used here with an optative/assuring sense ('will be' / 'may be'); جهان‌آفرین ('world-creator') follows for emphasis as the noun/predicate head.
segments.s110-g81: هرانکس که زنده است is a relative clause: هرانکس ('whoever/everyone who') + که (relative 'who') + زنده است (present tense 'is alive'). ز here is the poetic form of از ('of/from'), so ز ایرانیان means 'from among the Iranians.' The whole segment therefore picks out 'whomever among the Iranians is alive.'
segments.s110-g82: بیارم و ببندم are two first‑person present/subjunctive verbs in sequence ('I will bring' / 'I will bind' or 'I bring / I bind'); the direct object is omitted but links back to the people named in the previous segment. کمر بر میان is a locative phrase: کمر 'waist/girdle' + بر 'on/upon' + میان 'the middle.' The verb بیارم here is the classical form of بیاورم ('I bring'), and the clause means 'I will bring (them) and bind a girdle on the middle (of their waists).'
segments.s110-g83: نه ... نه is the repeated negation used to list what the speaker is not. مانم is the 1st‑person present/archaic use of ماندن ('I remain / I am'), functioning here like a copula ('I am not Arzhang'). دیو سپید is noun + adjective (adjective follows noun in Persian): 'the white demon.'
segments.s110-g84: This continues the negative list with further names/terms after repeated نه. The items appear as bare nouns (proper names or titles) coordinated by the repeated نه. The phrase structure is simply a sequence of negated nominal items ('not X, not Y...').
segments.s110-g85: به نام is the fixed prepositional phrase 'in the name of.' جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator' and یک خدای ('one God') is an emphatic/indefinite nominal phrase placed after it. Word order is preposition + name + appositive noun phrase ('in the name of the Creator of the world, one God').
segments.s110-g86: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'یک خدای' ('who ...'). نگرداند is negative 3rd‑person singular (with ن‑) of گردانیدن/گرداندن ('to cause to turn / to allow/let' in context) and functions as 'will not let / does not cause.' از marks separation or source ('from'), so از رخش پای literally 'the foot from Rakhsh' — idiomatically 'lift his foot from Rakhsh.' The clause therefore reads 'who will not let Rostam lift/withdraw his foot from Rakhsh.'
segments.s110-g87: مگر here means 'unless' (introducing an exception). دست ارژنگ بسته is a noun phrase with the past participle/adjective بسته 'bound/tied' postposed: 'Arzhang's hand (being) bound.' چو سنگ is a simile 'like a stone.' The whole segment supplies the conditional exception 'unless Arzhang's hand, bound like a stone...'
segments.s110-g88: فگنده is a past participle (thrown/cast) modifying an implied object (what was thrown); به گردنش is the prepositional phrase 'around his neck' (گردن + the clitic ش = 'his'); در پالهنگ gives location or instrument 'into/into the palahng' (palahng = leopard‑skin cloak). The sequence describes the action that would constitute the exception: '(had) thrown (it) around his neck into the palahng.'
segments.s110-g1: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase meaning “after that/thereupon.” جهانجوی خسته is a compound noun phrase: جهانجوی (‘world‑seeking’) modified by the adjective خسته (‘weary’). جگر here is the noun that will serve as the object/topic of the following verb (it names the inner organ/seat of feeling). The whole segment functions as a topicalized object phrase placed before the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s110-g2: برون کرد is a past action: برون (out/forth) + کرد (did/made) = “brought out/pulled out.” The subject noun مردی follows the verb (inverted word order typical in poetry: O V S), so “a man” (or ‘he’) is the agent. چو introduces a simile “like/as,” and مرغی به پر completes that simile: مرغی ‘a bird’ + به پر ‘with/by a feather’ (instrumental/associative), i.e. ‘like a bird with its feather’ or more naturally ‘as a bird plucking a feather.’
segments.s110-g3: سوی زابلستان is a directional prepositional phrase ‘toward Zabulistan.’ فرستاد is the simple past ‘sent/dispatched.’ زود is an adverb ‘soon/quickly’ modifying فرستاد; in this word order the adverb may follow the verb for metrical/poetic reasons but still modifies the sending.
segments.s110-g4: به نزدیک is another directional/locative phrase ‘to/near.’ دستان here appears as a place/name (contextually ‘Dastān’) and functions as the target location; none of the enumerated lexical senses exactly match a proper‑name reading, so it is treated as a toponym in context. و joins the next phrase; رستم is the addressee (proper name) and درود is a noun ‘greeting’ or ‘salutation’ (object of the sending). The whole segment specifies destination/recipient and the greeting sent.
segments.s110-g5: کنون is an adverb ‘now.’ چشم is used as ‘sight/eye’ (the faculty of seeing) and is the grammatical subject. شد is the past of شدن ‘to become,’ so چشم شد تیره = ‘his sight became dark/dim.’ The coordinating و links a second predicate تیره‑بخت, an adjectival compound ‘ill‑fated/unlucky,’ so the clause states two related states of misfortune.
segments.s110-g6: به خاک اندر آمد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘came down into/to the earth/dust’: به + خاک expresses direction/toward the ground, اندر ‘in/into’ strengthens the locative, and آمد is the past ‘came.’ سر تاج و تخت is a coordinated nominal group (‘head, crown, and throne’) following the verb; in poetic inversion the patient/theme (the symbols of rule) comes after the verb, conveying that head/crown/throne have fallen to the earth.
segments.s110-g7: جگر خسته is a noun phrase ‘weary heart/liver’ (jigar as the seat of feeling). در چنگ is a prepositional phrase ‘in the clutch/grip,’ where در = ‘in’ and چنگ = ‘claw/grip.’ آهرمنم is the name Ahriman with the enclitic -م; syntactically this marks the demon as the possessor/agent of the grip — understood as ‘in the grip of Ahriman (on me).’
segments.s110-g8: همی is a classical adverb meaning ‘continually/still’ and commonly appears with present/stative verb forms. بگسلد is a present/habitual form (ب‑ + گسلد) meaning ‘rends/tears apart’ (here intransitive/causative sense of pulling away). زار is an adverbial/qualifying word ‘wretchedly/agonizingly,’ modifying the verb. جان is ‘soul/life,’ از marks separation ‘from,’ and تنم is ‘my body’ (تن + enclitic -م). The clause as a whole says the heart in the demon’s grip keeps tearing the soul away from the speaker’s body.
segments.s110-g57: اگرچه is a concessive conjunction meaning 'although'/'even though'. به رنجست is a compressed poetic sequence: رنجست = رنج است ('it is hardship/suffering'), with به functioning adverbially here (stylistic, 'with/in hardship'); هم is an emphatic particle ('still/even so'); بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form (poetic ب‑ prefix) of گذشتن, used here as a general/future-oriented 'passes' or 'will pass'. The whole clause gives a concessive frame: 'Although it is hardship, (it) will pass.'
segments.s110-g58: پی here means 'after'/'following' and heads a prepositional/relational phrase. رخش فرخ is a noun + epithet (رخش = steed, فرخ = fortunate/auspicious) and زمین is the direct object of بسپرد; بسپرد is the archaic/poetic present of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over'. Grammatically: 'Following his fortunate steed, he/it will entrust/hand over the earth.'
segments.s110-g59: تا means 'until' here and introduces a temporal clause. شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'dark night'. برکشد is the poetic present/subjunctive of برکشیدن/برکشیدن ('to lift/draw up' or 'to break (dawn)'), so تا برکشد روز چاک = 'until the dark night lifts and the day is torn/breaks open' (i.e. until dawn). روز چاک places چاک ('split/torn') after روز as a poetic adjective.
segments.s110-g60: نیایش is a noun 'prayer/supplication'. کنم is the first-person present/subjunctive of کردن used here to mean 'I do/offer' (i.e. 'I will pray' or 'let me pray'). پیش takes the sense 'in the presence of/before' and governs یزدان پاک ('pure God'), so the clause literally reads 'I will offer prayer before the pure God.'
segments.s110-g61: مگر here functions as 'unless'/'except that' in a wishful or conditional sense. باز means 'again'. بینم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of دیدن ('may I see' / 'I see again' as a wish). بر و یال تو are two coordinated nouns 'بر' + 'یال' with the possessive تو applying to both; in this line بر is a noun meaning a part of the head/fore (poetic usage) rather than the preposition (so its listed prepositional senses do not apply).
segments.s110-g62: همان is the demonstrative 'that very'/'the same'. پهلوی is 'side'/'flank' and heads a noun phrase further specified by چنگ و گوپال — two coordinated items describing the flank. چنگ here takes the sense 'claw/talon' (poetic image), while گوپال is used as a proper name/epithet (Gopala) rather than the common noun 'cowherd'; تو at the end is the possessive 'your'. Grammatically the string lists features of 'that same flank' possessed by the addressee.
segments.s110-g63: وگر is the conditional conjunction 'and if'/'but if'. هوش تو is 'your intellect/wit' with the enclitic تو. نیز means 'also'. بر دست دیو is a prepositional phrase 'into/on the hand/power of the demon' (بر + دست used idiomatically as 'in the power of'); together the clause reads 'if your wits too come into the demon's hand.'
segments.s110-g64: برآید is the poetic present of برآمدن meaning 'to arise/happen/come about' and here functions impersonally ('it will come to pass'). به فرمان is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by the command/decree of'. گیهان خدیو is a genitive phrase 'the ruler (خدیو) of the world (گیهان)' or 'the world's ruler'; خدیو is used as a title 'ruler/viceroy'. So: 'it will come to pass by the command of the world's ruler.'
segments.s110-g9: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. از پندهای تو is a prepositional phrase ('of/ about your counsels') serving as the complement of the verb یادآورم; یادآورم here is the fused verb یاد + آورم ('I bring to mind / I recall') in a habitual/present sense (the habitual aspect is reinforced by همی in the next segment). Word order is typical poetic inversion: conjunction + prepositional object + verb.
segments.s110-g10: همی is a habitual/progressive marker meaning 'always/continually' placed before the verb phrase. از جگر سرد is an ablative phrase 'from my liver/heart' where جگر (the bodily organ used idiomatically = seat of feeling) is followed by the adjective سرد. باد آورم is a present habitual verb complex 'I bring/produce a wind' (آورم = 'I bring'); literally 'from my cold heart I bring (forth) a wind.'
segments.s110-g11: نرفتم is the past negative first-person singular of رفتن ('I did not go / I did not follow'). به گفتار تو is the prepositional complement 'to/ toward your speech' (i.e., 'your counsel'), and هوشمند is a postpositive adjective modifying گفتار ('your wise speech/counsel'). The clause means 'I did not follow your wise counsel.'
segments.s110-g12: ز introduces a causal phrase here ('because of / from'), taking ز + noun. کم دانشی = 'insufficient knowledge / lack of knowledge' (کم modifies دانشی). بر من آمد گزند is a verbal clause in which گزند ('harm') is the subject and آمد ('came') is the past verb; بر من marks the experiencer 'upon me' so the whole means 'harm came upon me / befell me.'
segments.s110-g13: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). تو is the subject; نبندی is the second-person singular negative present/subjunctive of بندن/بستن here meaning 'you do not bind / you do not confine / you do not set.' بدین بد میان is a compact poetic phrase: بدین functions as 'with/ by this' (formed with the prefix ب- + دین in older usage) and میان means 'among.' Together بدین بد میان = 'this ill among (other) ills' (i.e. 'confine this ill among evils'). Word order places the locative phrase after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s110-g14: همه سود را uses را as the definite/object marker on 'all profit/gain.' مایه باشد زیان is a predicative clause: مایه 'source/cause' followed by باشد (present/jussive copula 'will be / is') and زیان 'harm.' Together: 'all gain will be a source of harm.'
segments.s110-g15: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' پوینده is the nominative 'the seeker' (agent). نزدیک دستان is a locative phrase 'near the hands' (دستان here is literal 'hands' in this line), and رسید is the past verb 'arrived/reached' — together 'when the seeker came near the hands.' Word order follows the common poetic pattern subject + locative adjunct + verb.
segments.s110-g16: بگفت is the past-reporting verb 'he said / he told.' آنچ is the relative/indefinite pronoun 'that which / what,' governing the following coordinated verbs دانست و دید و شنید (all past tense): 'knew and saw and heard.' The whole clause means 'he told what he knew and had seen and heard.'
segments.s110-g89: This is the object phrase: 'سر و مغز پولاد را' is a coordinated noun phrase ('the head and the core of the steel') marked by the direct‑object marker را. Poetic Persian often omits the ezāfe between nouns (here between مغز and پولاد). 'زیر پای' is a locative prepositional phrase ('under the foot') that attaches to this object and indicates where the steel was placed.
segments.s110-g90: This completes the clause: 'زیر پای پی رخش' = 'under the foot/track of Rakhsh' (the line break split the phrase). برده is a past participle/past form meaning 'has taken/has put' and governs the following object 'زمین را' (the earth). 'ز جای' is a small prepositional phrase 'from its place' showing what was displaced. Overall the verb applies to both the placing under the hoof and the uprooting of the ground.
segments.s110-g91: بپوشید is a past tense verb 'he put on' (3rd‑person singular). ببر here names the garment ('a tiger' i.e. tiger‑skin) as its object. و links this action to the next clause. برآورد is a past verb meaning 'brought up / raised' with یال ('mane') as its object; in poetic use it describes bristling or raising the mane.
segments.s110-g92: خواند is the past tense verb 'he cried/called/sang'; it precedes the subject زال here (verb-before-subject order is common in classical Persian). آفرین is an interjection 'Bravo! / well done!' and بسیاری modifies خواند to mean 'many times' or 'frequently'. The short word برو immediately before آفرین functions as part of the exclamation in the poem (an interjectional element) rather than the literal imperative 'go'.
segments.s110-g93: چو here means 'when'. رستم is the subject. برخش should be read as the preposition بر + رخش ('upon Rakhsh'); in the manuscript the words are run together poetically. اندر آورد is a verbal compound meaning 'brought in / set (his) foot' (آورد = 'brought, put'). پای is the direct object of this verb: 'he placed/set his foot (upon Rakhsh).' Note the poetic elision of the space between بر and رخش.
segments.s110-g94: This is a pair of short verbless/resultative phrases: 'رخش رنگ بر جای' literally 'Rakhsh’s color (رنگ) on (بر) its place (جای)' — an idiom meaning the horse recovered its color/appearance or composure. 'و دل هم به جای' parallels it: 'and the heart too (was) back in place' — i.e. courage/heart was restored. In classical Persian the copula is often omitted in such resultative expressions.
segments.s110-g95: بیامد is the past tense 'she came'. 'پر از آب' is a descriptive phrase 'full of water' (i.e. eyes full of tears). رودابه is the proper name (Rudabeh) and روی here refers to her face — the phrase orders are poetic: the descriptive phrase follows the verb and precedes the name/face.
segments.s110-g96: همی is an adverb meaning 'still/continuously' or an intensifier here. زار modifies the verb as 'wailing/woeful' and بگریست is the past 'she wept/wailed'. دستان بروی should be read as 'دستان بر روی (روی)' = '(her) hands upon (her) face' — a fused prepositional form that in the word list appears as بروی but here functions as the preposition 'on/over'.
segments.s110-g97: بدو is the prepositional pronoun meaning “to him/her” placed before the verb (poetic word order). گفت is the past verb “said” (here used after the prepositional pronoun). کای is the archaic vocative particle “O,” followed by مادر نیکخوی, a noun + adjective phrase “mother, of good disposition,” addressing the addressee directly.
segments.s110-g98: نه negates the following verb. بگزیدم is the past 1st‑person form “I chose” (here negated: “I did not choose”); in classical Persian the b‑ prefix appears on past stems. این راه is the object “this path.” برآرزوی is an adverbial/ prepositional phrase “for/by (one's) desire / in hope of,” attached to the verb to indicate motive.
segments.s110-g99: مرا (here split in the data as م + را) is the accusative object “me” (را marks definiteness). در غم خود = “in your/one's own sorrow” (خود is reflexive/possessive: ‘your own’ referring to the addressee). گذاری همی is the present/habitual 2nd‑person form “you place/you leave” with the continuous/archaic aspect marker همی “still/continually.” The verb is placed after the object phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s110-g100: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase “in/to God” indicating the object of reliance. چه is the interrogative “what” (asking about the kind/amount of hope). امیدداری is the present 2nd‑person form “you have hope / you hope” (compound noun + داشتن as a verbal form). همی again gives continuous emphasis (“do you still have / do you ever have hope?”). The clause asks rhetorically about reliance on God.
segments.s110-g101: چنین “thus/so” introduces the result. آمدم is a perfect/past form meaning “came to me” (the final -م is a dative/enclitic ‘to me’ here, so the sense is passive/experiential: ‘came to me’ rather than ‘I came’). بخشش روزگار = “the mercy/forgiveness (or gift) of fate” — a nominal phrase functioning as the thing that came.
segments.s110-g102: تو is the vocative/emphatic “you.” جان و تن من = “my life and body” (possessive idea expressed by the following من). به زنهار دار is an imperative/request: زنهار “beware / take heed” used adverbially with به, and دار here functions as an imperative “hold/keep” (classical usage), i.e. “keep (them) — take heed / I beseech you.” The phrase is a plea for preservation.
segments.s110-g103: به + verbal noun (پدرود کردنش) expresses purpose: “to do his farewell” = “to bid him farewell”; ش is the third‑person clitic “his/him.” رفتند is the past 3rd‑person‑plural “they went.” پیش here means “to/into (his) presence” or “forward.” The whole segment: “They went forward to bid him farewell.”
segments.s110-g104: که introduces a subordinate clause (“for / because / that”). دانست is past “he knew.” کش is an archaic/poetic element here (part of a compound or agentive/relative element in classical usage) linked to the following predicate; باز is the adverb “again.” بینند is present/subjunctive 3rd‑person‑plural “they see / may see,” used in reported speech after دانست. بیش is an intensifier “more.” Together the clause states what he knew: that they would see (him) again, more.

sentences.s110-s53: Thus time keeps passing, and the wise man counts its moments.
sentences.s110-s54: Every ill day that passed because of you was the very instant from which the world became prosperous.
sentences.s110-s17: Verily, the Lord reared you for these times.
sentences.s110-s18: It is not fitting for such an Ahrimanic deed that you should find ease — even if you draw breath.
sentences.s110-s19: Make your bearing known boldly to the tiger; make your statement harsh and firm.
sentences.s110-s20: Every man whose eye beheld the tip of your spear will say that his spirit has departed.
sentences.s110-s9: Both that treasure and that famed army were arrayed like a flower in spring.
sentences.s110-s10: He entrusted the whole turning world to the diwan, as if one would say the wind had come to carry it off.
sentences.s110-s11: When he heard it, he tore the skin from his body; he had hidden this from the enemy and likewise from his friend.
sentences.s110-s12: The clear‑hearted one saw ill‑omens from afar, wondering what will befall the age from now on.
sentences.s110-s37: But the earlier great ones had no counsel to descend into hell at anyone's feet.
sentences.s110-s38: No one so ruined in body and disposition comes, sated, before a ravening lion.
sentences.s110-s39: Now I am girded and resolute; I will accept aid from no one except a just helper.
sentences.s110-s40: I will sacrifice body and life for the spahbed; I will break the enchanters' spell on the heart.
sentences.s110-s33: Can anyone hold back this speech? If someone sets it down thus, it must be set down.
sentences.s110-s34: It will not remain here for anyone; they will read it, even if it endures long.
sentences.s110-s35: No one who made the world famed by a lofty name will be base in death.
sentences.s110-s36: Thus Rostam said to Farrokh his father: 'I have girded myself at the order of my belt.'
sentences.s110-s21: If you wage war on the sea, blood will be shed; at your cry the mountains will turn into a plain.
sentences.s110-s22: Arzhang and the white demon must never have any hope of taking your life.
sentences.s110-s23: Now, (break) the neck of the king of Mazandaran; with the heavy mace, shatter all his wit.
sentences.s110-s24: Thus Rostam gave this answer: 'The road is long, and if I become vengeful I will seek revenge.'
sentences.s110-s13: Sam, mighty of hand, said to Rostam thus: that the sword has grown short in its sheath.
sentences.s110-s14: It is not fitting that henceforth I should lounge and graze, or else that we should rear the throne ourselves.
sentences.s110-s15: For the king of the world is in the dragon's breath — what sort of calamity is upon the Iranians?
sentences.s110-s16: Now you must saddle Rakhsh, and take up the world‑bestowing sword of vengeance.
sentences.s110-s25: Zal told him this about the kingship: there are two routes, and both are attended by hardship and burden.
sentences.s110-s26: One of the two ways is the one Kavus went; the other is mountains and heights and a journey of two sevens (i.e. fourteen stages).
sentences.s110-s27: It is full of demons and of lions and full of darkness; your eye will remain fixed in astonishment at it.
sentences.s110-s28: You, make a brief choice and behold the marvel, for the world's Creator will be your ally.
sentences.s110-s41: I will bring every living Iranian and gird a belt about his waist.
sentences.s110-s42: I am neither Arzhang nor the white demon, nor Sanjeh, nor Polad Ghendi, nor Bid.
sentences.s110-s43: In the name of the Creator of the world, the one God, who will not let Rostam lift his foot from Rakhsh.
sentences.s110-s44: Unless Arzhang's hand, bound like a stone, had thrown it around his neck into the palahng (leopard‑skin).
sentences.s110-s1: Thereafter the weary, world‑seeking man pulled out his heart (liver) like a bird plucking a feather.
sentences.s110-s2: He quickly dispatched (him) toward Zabulistan, to near Dastān, bearing greetings for Rostam.
sentences.s110-s3: Now his sight grew dark and ill‑fated; the head — crown and throne — came down to the dust.
sentences.s110-s4: My weary heart, caught in Ahriman’s grip, continually rends my soul wretchedly from my body.
sentences.s110-s29: Even though it is hardship, it will pass; following his fortunate steed, he will hand over the earth.
sentences.s110-s30: Until the dark night lifts and the day breaks open, I will offer prayer before the pure God.
sentences.s110-s31: Unless I see again your brow and mane — that very flank, your claw and Gopala —
sentences.s110-s32: And if your wit also falls into the hand of a demon, it will come to pass by the decree of the world's ruler.
sentences.s110-s5: When I recall your counsels, a cold wind comes from my heart.
sentences.s110-s6: I did not heed your wise words; from lack of knowledge harm befell me.
sentences.s110-s7: If you do not confine this ill among evils, every gain will be a source of loss.
sentences.s110-s8: When the seeker drew near the hands, he told what he knew and had seen and heard.
sentences.s110-s45: He thrust the steel’s head and core beneath Rakhsh’s foot and tore the earth from its place.
sentences.s110-s46: He put on the tiger (skin) and raised his mane; Zal cried out 'Bravo!' many times.
sentences.s110-s47: When Rostam set his foot on Rakhsh, Rakhsh’s color returned to its place — and so did his heart.
sentences.s110-s48: Rudabeh came with her face full of water; she wailed and wept, her hands upon her face.
sentences.s110-s49: He said to her, “O kind mother, I did not choose this path out of my own desire.”
sentences.s110-s50: You leave me in your sorrow continually; what hope do you place in God?
sentences.s110-s51: Thus came to me the mercy of fate; you, keep my life and body — take heed (I beg you).
sentences.s110-s52: They went forward to bid him farewell, for he knew they would see him again more.
